metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a RETIRE step for the `pre-a3-remediation` tag: after the live run is declared successful and the GHA schedule is re-enabled, document (and script) `git tag -d pre-a3-remediation && git push origin :refs/tags/pre-a3-remediation` so the shared repo doesn't accumulate stale remediation tags.
findings.finding: The `pre-a3-remediation` git tag has no defined RETIRE/cleanup path; it will accumulate permanently in the shared repo.
findings.location: Safety section — 'git -C <tracker> tag pre-a3-remediation <oid>'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Post-window cleanup' sub-step under Safety: 'After confirming 0 unresolved faults and re-enabling the schedule, delete the tag: `git -C <tracker> tag -d pre-a3-remediation && git -C <tracker> push origin :refs/tags/pre-a3-remediation`.' Document that this step is skipped only if rollback was invoked.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `tests/golden/cli_help/fsck__help.out` and `tests/golden/cli_help/fsck__dashhelp.out` (and any related overview golden files that include the fsck usage line) to the Scope as edit targets requiring update when `--repair`/`--dry-run`/`--limit` flags are added.
findings.finding: The plan adds `--repair`, `--dry-run`, and `--limit` flags to `fsck_cli` in `_commands/fsck.py`. The current CLI help golden files (`tests/golden/cli_help/fsck__help.out` and `tests/golden/cli_help/fsck__dashhelp.out`) are pinned to the current `Usage: rebar fsck [--output json]` signature. These golden files are consumers outside `_commands/` that will break when the new flags are added, but they are not enumerated in the plan's Scope.
findings.location: Scope section: '`_commands/fsck.py` (`--repair`/`--dry-run`/`--limit` ...)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope: 'Update `tests/golden/cli_help/fsck__help.out`, `tests/golden/cli_help/fsck__dashhelp.out`, and overview golden files to reflect the new `--repair`/`--dry-run`/`--limit` flags.'

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the retire helper's exact function signature and canonical module location (not just 'compact.py') that A1 must deliver for A3 to import, so the A1→A3 interface contract is verifiable.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'The retire helper is invoked from the `fsck --repair` path (shared with A1's rename logic)' and lists `_commands/compact.py (shared retire helper)` in Scope, implying a `retire` helper already exists in `compact.py` that A3 merely calls. Reading `compact.py` in full reveals no retire function, no `.retired` rename logic, and no such helper anywhere. This is new code A1 must deliver; the plan describes it as if it exists.
findings.location: Repair per fault class — SNAPSHOT_INCONSISTENT bullet; Scope section
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope or a new 'Interface contract' subsection: 'A1 must expose `retire_event_file(path: str) -> None` (or equivalent) in `_commands/compact.py`; A3 imports it as `from rebar._commands.compact import retire_event_file`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a bridging rule in the ORPHAN_EVENT repair step specifying that orphans whose event type is in the HUMAN TRIAGE list are SKIPPED from auto-rebuild (logged with event UUID and type for human triage) and only AUTO-RECOVER-typed orphans proceed to the A2 rebuild path.
findings.finding: The orphan disposition policy distinguishes AUTO-RECOVER vs HUMAN TRIAGE event types, but the ORPHAN_EVENT repair step says unconditionally 'A2's reduce_ticket(…, include_retired=True) rebuild -> re-write snapshot' for all 11 orphans. An executor cannot determine whether orphans with HUMAN TRIAGE event types (STATUS, EDIT, SIGNATURE, etc.) should be rebuilt automatically or skipped/logged separately — the two sections conflict.
findings.location: 'Repair per fault class' section (ORPHAN_EVENT bullet) vs 'Orphan disposition policy' section
findings.suggested_fix: In the ORPHAN_EVENT bullet, split into two sub-steps: (1) classify each orphan by event type against the disposition policy; (2) AUTO-RECOVER types -> A2 rebuild; HUMAN TRIAGE types -> log to triage file (same format as MISSING_CREATE), count as 'pending human review' for AC6 purposes.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify A3's compact.py edit scope: if A3 only *calls* A1's retire helper (no new code added to compact.py by A3), remove compact.py from A3's Scope as an edit target and enumerate it as a consumed dependency instead.
findings.finding: `_commands/compact.py` is listed as an A3 Scope edit target for a 'shared retire helper', but A1 (tricolour-head-ratfish) already fully owns all compact.py changes (it adds the `.retired` rename + `RETIRED_SUFFIX`). A3 owning a separate compact.py edit creates an overlapping-ownership ambiguity: it is unclear whether A3 adds a new exported function to compact.py or merely *calls* A1's already-added helper. If the former, the two sibling plans both write compact.py with no sequencing guarantee; if the latter, compact.py should be enumerated as a consumed (read) target, not an edited one.
findings.location: Scope section: '`_commands/compact.py` (shared retire helper)'
findings.suggested_fix: Change Scope to: '`_commands/fsck.py` (`--repair`/`--dry-run`/`--limit` + per-batch git commit + local marker + atomic per-snapshot rewrite + retire-still-present). Calls A1's retire helper from `_commands/compact.py` (read-only dependency, not an A3 edit).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the ad-hoc `<snap>.tmp` + `os.rename` description in the Safety section with an explicit call to `rebar._store.fsutil.atomic_write`, consistent with how `compact.py` already writes SNAPSHOT files.
findings.finding: The Safety section specifies 'per-snapshot replace is atomic (write `<snap>.tmp`, `os.rename` over old)' as if it requires a new ad-hoc implementation, but `rebar._store.fsutil.atomic_write` already provides exactly this contract (same-dir temp + `os.replace`) and is used in `compact.py` itself for SNAPSHOT writes. Describing a bespoke `<snap>.tmp` + `os.rename` pattern risks reimplementing functionality already centralized in `fsutil.atomic_write`.
findings.location: Safety section: 'per-snapshot replace is atomic (write `<snap>.tmp`, `os.rename` over old)'
findings.suggested_fix: In `fsck --repair`'s ORPHAN_EVENT snapshot-rewrite path, call `fsutil.atomic_write(snap_path, canonical_str(new_snapshot), encoding='utf-8')` directly instead of rolling a bespoke temp-rename.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the bespoke `write <snap>.tmp` + `os.rename` pattern in the per-snapshot atomic rewrite with `fsutil.atomic_write(snap_path, ...)` (the established codebase utility), and confirm the plan references `os.replace`-semantics (not `os.rename`) everywhere a rename-over-existing is required.
findings.finding: The Safety section specifies 'write `<snap>.tmp`, `os.rename` over old' for the per-snapshot atomic rewrite, reinventing `fsutil.atomic_write` (already used by compact.py, txn.py, hlc.py, etc.) and using `os.rename` instead of the codebase's established `os.replace` (which handles rename-over-existing atomically on all platforms; `os.rename` raises OSError if the destination exists on some platforms).
findings.location: Safety section: 'Per-snapshot replace is atomic (write `<snap>.tmp`, `os.rename` over old)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Safety section, replace: 'Per-snapshot replace is atomic (write `<snap>.tmp`, `os.rename` over old)' with: 'Per-snapshot replace is atomic via `fsutil.atomic_write(snap_path, canonical_str(repaired_snapshot))` (the established codebase utility; uses `os.replace` internally).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC5) to assert that AUTO-RECOVER event types (COMMENT, LINK, UNLINK, TAG_DELTA, COMMITS) are rebuilt automatically and HUMAN TRIAGE types (STATUS, EDIT, FILE_IMPACT, VERIFY_COMMANDS, SIGNATURE) are not written and are logged/flagged, covering the orphan disposition policy as a tested deliverable.
findings.finding: The 'Orphan disposition policy' section describes a material deliverable — routing ORPHAN_EVENT sub-types (COMMENT, LINK, UNLINK, TAG_DELTA, COMMITS → AUTO-RECOVER; STATUS, EDIT, FILE_IMPACT, VERIFY_COMMANDS, SIGNATURE → HUMAN TRIAGE) — but no acceptance criterion explicitly covers testing or verifying this routing logic.
findings.location: 'Orphan disposition policy' section vs AC1–AC6

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the ORPHAN_EVENT repair flow for tickets containing HUMAN TRIAGE event types: specify whether (a) AUTO-RECOVER events are applied immediately and HUMAN TRIAGE events are deferred (partial rebuild), (b) the entire ticket is held until human triage completes, or (c) another disposition; and state explicitly how such tickets are counted toward the 'zero unresolved' AC.
findings.finding: The ORPHAN_EVENT repair path does not specify how tickets whose orphaned events include HUMAN TRIAGE event types (STATUS, EDIT, etc.) are handled — whether their snapshot rewrite is blocked pending triage, partially applied with only AUTO-RECOVER events, or skipped entirely — leaving it ambiguous how these tickets contribute to the 'drive to zero' goal.
findings.location: Repair per fault class (ORPHAN_EVENT) and Orphan disposition policy sections

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a co-located decision rule in the 'Repair per fault class / ORPHAN_EVENT' section that explicitly states: inspect the orphan event's `event_type` (parseable from filename suffix); if it is in AUTO-RECOVER set → invoke `reduce_ticket(..., include_retired=True)` rebuild; if in HUMAN TRIAGE set → log disposition and skip auto-write.
findings.finding: The ORPHAN_EVENT repair algorithm unconditionally invokes rebuild for all orphans, but the disposition policy classifies some orphan event types (STATUS, EDIT, FILE_IMPACT, VERIFY_COMMANDS, SIGNATURE) as HUMAN TRIAGE with no auto-rebuild. The processing protocol that routes a given orphan event to auto-rebuild vs human-triage — i.e., parse the orphan's event_type from its filename, look it up in the disposition table, and branch — is never stated in the repair code spec or co-located with the schema that needs it.
findings.location: Repair per fault class — ORPHAN_EVENT; Orphan disposition policy
findings.suggested_fix: Under 'Repair per fault class / ORPHAN_EVENT', add: 'Routing: parse `event_type` from the orphan filename suffix. If `event_type` ∈ {COMMENT, LINK, UNLINK, TAG_DELTA, COMMITS} → AUTO-RECOVER (rebuild). If `event_type` ∈ {STATUS, EDIT, FILE_IMPACT, VERIFY_COMMANDS, SIGNATURE} → HUMAN TRIAGE (log + skip, no write). Unknown type → HUMAN TRIAGE by default.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Create (or update) a persistent ops runbook / CLAUDE.md section that captures: the rollback command sequence, reconciler-pause/resume steps, and the orphan disposition policy table — referenced from the PR rather than duplicated in it.
findings.finding: The rollback procedure, reconciler-pause/resume steps, and orphan disposition policy (AUTO-RECOVER vs HUMAN TRIAGE event-type list) are only captured in plan prose. After the PR merges, this operational knowledge has no persistent home (ops runbook, ADR, or CLAUDE.md update), making future operators unable to reproduce or audit the remediation safely.
findings.location: Safety section; Orphan disposition policy section; Acceptance Criteria AC-2 and AC-3
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `docs/ops/a3-remediation-runbook.md` (or equivalent) that is committed to the repo alongside the code changes, containing: (1) pre-flight checklist, (2) rollback procedure, (3) reconciler pause/resume steps, (4) orphan disposition policy table. Reference it from the PR description.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add AC / synthetic-fixture tests asserting that a mid-repair failure (reduce_ticket exception, partial source-file retire, failed git push after commit) leaves the affected ticket's snapshot unchanged and re-flagged by the next fsck run — covering the 'per-ticket failure logs+skips (no batch abort)' invariant explicitly.
findings.finding: The testing plan covers the happy path for ORPHAN_EVENT rebuild (reduce_ticket succeeds, snapshot is rewritten) and SNAPSHOT_INCONSISTENT retire (all sources retired), but has no AC or test asserting that a mid-repair failure (e.g., reduce_ticket raises, a source-file retire fails partway, or a batch git push fails) leaves the affected ticket's snapshot UNCHANGED and re-processable by the next fsck run. The plan states 'per-ticket failure logs+skips (no batch abort)' but this invariant is untested, which is a missing failure-path test on new user-facing repair flows.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC 4 (resumability) and AC 5 (synthetic-fixture tests); 'Safety' section ('per-ticket failure logs+skips (no batch abort)')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC 5: 'A fixture test injects a failure (a) after reduce_ticket raises mid-rebuild, (b) after the first of N source-file retires raises, and (c) after a simulated git-push failure post-commit; in each case the test asserts the ticket's snapshot file is byte-identical to its pre-repair state and that a subsequent fsck re-run still flags it.' For the push-failure case, document whether a committed-but-unpushed batch is considered safe (the pre-tag still covers it) or whether the push must be retried before the pre-tag is advanced.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit interface-contract assertion: confirm A2's ticket specifies `reduce_ticket(ticket_dir, include_retired: bool = False)` or equivalent, and add an AC-gating check that the parameter exists before A3 is approved.
findings.finding: The plan specifies ORPHAN_EVENT repair as 'A2's `reduce_ticket(..., include_retired=True)` rebuild -> re-write snapshot'. The current `reduce_ticket` signature is `reduce_ticket(ticket_dir_path: str | os.PathLike[str])` — a single positional arg with no `include_retired` parameter. `prepare_event_files` in `_cache.py` globs only `*.json` and filters on `endswith('.json')`, so `.retired` files are excluded by extension. The `include_retired=True` calling convention is future A2 work, but the plan describes it as a usable interface contract.
findings.location: Repair per fault class — ORPHAN_EVENT bullet

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit post-rollback state assertion to the plan: specify that after `git reset --hard pre-a3-remediation && push --force-with-lease`, a `fsck` re-run must report fault counts equal to the pre-remediation baseline (2422+11+1), and add this as a concrete verification step in AC2's rollback rehearsal fixture.
findings.finding: The plan specifies the rollback command (`git reset --hard pre-a3-remediation && push --force-with-lease`) but never asserts the store's schema/data STATE after rollback completes — specifically, that fsck post-rollback reports the same pre-remediation fault counts (2422 SNAPSHOT_INCONSISTENT + 11 ORPHAN_EVENT + 1 MISSING_CREATE) and zero new faults. The T11 overlay requires asserting post-rollback state, not merely that the rollback command exits 0. Since `.retired` renames and snapshot rewrites are committed via `git add -A`, they are reverted by `reset --hard`; but the plan does not explicitly verify or assert this invariant, leaving an executor to assume reversibility without a concrete check.
findings.location: Safety section — 'rollback = git -C <tracker> reset --hard pre-a3-remediation && git -C <tracker> push --force-with-lease'; AC2 — 'rollback (reset --hard + --force-with-lease) documented AND rehearsed on a fixture (asserts post-rollback store == pre-tag state)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Safety section, add: 'Post-rollback verification: `fsck` re-run on the rolled-back store must report exactly the pre-remediation fault counts (2422 SNAPSHOT_INCONSISTENT, 11 ORPHAN_EVENT, 1 MISSING_CREATE) and zero new fault types. The rollback rehearsal fixture (AC2) must run this check and assert equality.' In AC2, replace 'asserts post-rollback store == pre-tag state' with 'asserts post-rollback fsck fault counts == pre-tag baseline AND git diff HEAD pre-a3-remediation -- tickets/ is empty.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for 'no in-flight pass', e.g.: `gh run list --workflow=reconcile-bridge.yml --status=in_progress --json databaseId | jq -e '. == []'` (exit 0 = safe to proceed), and gate the repair start on this check.
findings.finding: AC3 ('verified no in-flight pass') has no concrete proving command. An executor has no specified mechanism to confirm the reconciler is not mid-flight before beginning writes.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3: 'Reconciler paused by disabling the GHA schedule (verified no in-flight pass); re-enabled after.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document and implement retry/backoff (e.g., up to 3 attempts with exponential backoff) for each per-batch `git push`; specify operator action (pause, inspect, resume or rollback) when all retries are exhausted.
findings.finding: The per-batch git commit+push sequence has no documented retry/backoff or recovery path for a push failure. If push fails mid-remediation (network blip, branch-protection rejection, auth expiry), local commits accumulate ahead of remote with no specified operator action short of full rollback, which discards all completed batches.
findings.location: Safety section — `git -C <tracker> add -A && git -C <tracker> commit -m 'a3-remediation: batch N/M' && git -C <tracker> push`
findings.suggested_fix: After each `git push` in the batch loop, retry up to 3 times with exponential backoff (5s, 15s, 45s). On final failure, log the local commit hash, stop the loop, and instruct the operator to either `git push` manually (resume) or `git reset --hard pre-a3-remediation && git push --force-with-lease` (rollback). Add a test that simulates push failure and asserts the loop halts and emits the recovery instruction.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Pin the exact A2 API contract that A3 will consume for ORPHAN_EVENT repair — either confirm `reduce_ticket(ticket_dir, include_retired=True)` is the precise signature A2 will deliver, or name the alternative entry point, so the A3 implementor has a concrete interface to code against.
findings.finding: The plan states the ORPHAN_EVENT repair uses 'A2's `reduce_ticket(..., include_retired=True)` rebuild -> re-write snapshot'. The current `reduce_ticket` signature is `def reduce_ticket(ticket_dir_path: str | os.PathLike[str]) -> dict | None` — it accepts no `include_retired` parameter. The plan depends on A2 adding this parameter. However, `reduce_ticket` has 40+ call sites across the codebase (confirmed by grep). The plan neither acknowledges the blast radius of the A2 signature change on those callers, nor enumerates that A3's `fsck --repair` ORPHAN path is the *only* new caller. If A2 uses a different mechanism (e.g., a separate function rather than a parameter on `reduce_ticket`), A3's invocation would be wrong.
findings.location: Repair per fault class section: 'ORPHAN_EVENT (11): ... A2's `reduce_ticket(..., include_retired=True)` rebuild'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Rewrite 'Repair per fault class' and Scope to be explicit that `--repair`, `--dry-run`, and `--limit` are NEW flags being added to `fsck_cli` (not extensions of existing stubs), and describe their argument-parsing contract.
findings.finding: The plan describes `fsck --repair` as an existing code path ('The retire helper is invoked from the `fsck --repair` path'), but `fsck.py` has no `--repair`, `--dry-run`, or `--limit` flags at all — `fsck_cli` parses only `--output`. All three are entirely new CLI surface, yet the plan's 'Repair per fault class' and 'Scope' sections use present-tense descriptions that imply these already exist as hooks to wire into.
findings.location: Repair per fault class / Scope sections
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope: '`fsck_cli` currently accepts only `--output`; `--repair`, `--dry-run`, and `--limit` must be added to the argv parsing loop. Specify: `--repair` enables writes; `--dry-run` (requires `--repair`) suppresses writes; `--limit=N` stops after N tickets.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Split AC6 into two measurable sub-criteria: (a) 'all AUTO-RECOVER orphans and SNAPSHOT_INCONSISTENT faults repaired — rebar fsck reports 0 auto-remediable faults' (in-session provable), and (b) 'all HUMAN TRIAGE items documented in ops/a3-triage.md and tracked in a follow-on ticket' (in-session provable by artifact existence), so sign-off on (a) does not block on (b).
findings.finding: AC6 may not be in-session completable if any of the 11 ORPHAN_EVENT faults carry HUMAN TRIAGE event types, because those require human decisions that cannot be made autonomously in the repair session — yet the criterion states 'live store reaches 0 UNRESOLVED faults' without accounting for how long human triage takes.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC6; 'Orphan disposition policy' section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a `--batch-delay` (or `--wait`) CLI parameter to `fsck --repair` so the inter-batch wait is configurable at runtime, consistent with how `--limit` is already exposed.
findings.finding: The inter-batch wait duration (`>=30s`) is not identified as a CLI-configurable parameter. If hardcoded, it is an operational threshold likely to need tuning based on actual git push latency and live-store load — a soon-to-change value that should be configurable.
findings.location: Safety section: 'Batched --limit=200, wait >=30s between'
findings.suggested_fix: In `_commands/fsck.py`, add `--batch-delay=30` (seconds, default 30) alongside `--limit`; document the default in the CLI help string.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the 'reject/abandon' exit from the dry-run review gate: document what the operator does when the dry-run output is unexpected (e.g., abort window, file a bug against the repair logic, re-run with `--exclude-ticket=<uuid>`, re-enable GHA schedule), so the gate has both a proceed and a cancel path.
findings.finding: The operator-facing dry-run review gate has no specified 'abandon/reject' exit: the plan says the live run is gated on human review of the dry-run output, but never defines what the operator should do if the diff looks wrong (cancel, escalate, file a bug, re-run with adjusted parameters).
findings.location: AC line 1 ('Live run is gated on human review') and Safety section ('Dry-run FIRST')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Strengthen the dual-write window specification: either add a technical enforcement mechanism (e.g., branch protection rule or a short-lived push block during the batch) OR add an explicit post-repair fsck re-run step that detects and re-repairs any tickets that received concurrent writes, and document this as part of the remediation runbook.
findings.finding: The dual-write window handling relies on social hold-off for manual writers during the repair window, and the plan claims 'a stray concurrent write merges rather than being lost.' However, the plan does not address the specific scenario where a manual push adds a new event to a ticket WHILE that ticket's snapshot is being repaired: after git merge, the merged state contains the new event AND the repaired snapshot computed WITHOUT that new event — the new event is now a sub-snapshot orphan of the same class (ORPHAN_EVENT) that A3 is trying to fix, creating new corruption. The plan's 'batched+committed design means a stray concurrent write merges rather than being lost' does not cover this correctness gap.
findings.location: Safety section — 'because enforcement is social, the batched+committed design means a stray concurrent write merges rather than being lost, and the pre-tag enables rollback'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how `N` and `M` in the batch commit message are computed: either (a) pre-scan all fsck output before any write to fix M, or (b) use `N` only (drop M) in the commit message since total is unknowable at batch-start.
findings.finding: The per-batch git commit message `'a3-remediation: batch N/M'` uses a denominator M (total batch count) whose computation site is never specified. With `--limit=200` the total M depends on knowing the full set of tickets to repair upfront, but the plan never states whether M is pre-computed before the first batch (requiring a full fsck scan pass), computed lazily (making M unknown until completion), or whether it should be omitted.
findings.location: Safety section — 'git -C <tracker> commit -m \'a3-remediation: batch N/M\''

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Make AC6 concrete: specify the proving command (e.g., `rebar fsck --json | jq -e '[.faults[] | select(.status=="UNRESOLVED")] | length == 0'`) and name the artifact where before/after counts are recorded (e.g., a table in the PR description or a committed `ops/a3-remediation-report.md`).
findings.finding: AC6's proving command is implied but unstated ('live store reaches 0 UNRESOLVED faults … before/after counts recorded'), and 'recorded' does not name a target artifact, making the criterion partially effort-framed rather than observable.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC6

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Align AC5's MISSING_CREATE language with the body: replace 'repair correctly' with 'logs the fault and routes to human triage (asserts zero file writes for this class)' to match the 'no automatic write' constraint stated in 'Repair per fault class'.
findings.finding: AC5 says MISSING_CREATE '...repair correctly' but the 'Repair per fault class' section explicitly states MISSING_CREATE = 'logs it and routes to human triage (no automatic write)'. These two characterizations contradict: a test asserting it 'repairs correctly' is undefined or misleading when no repair is performed.
findings.location: AC5 ('Synthetic-fixture unit tests: known SNAPSHOT_INCONSISTENT / ORPHAN_EVENT / MISSING_CREATE (incl. .retired) repair correctly') vs 'Repair per fault class' section (MISSING_CREATE entry)
findings.suggested_fix: AC5 revised fragment: '...MISSING_CREATE: asserts fault is logged and routed to human triage with zero automatic file writes...'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm the retire helper has at least 3 call-sites justifying placement in `compact.py`, or inline the rename at each of the two call-sites (A1's compact path and A3's fsck repair path) to avoid a premature abstraction.
findings.finding: The plan places the 'retire helper' (for renaming source files to `.retired`) in `_commands/compact.py` as a shared abstraction, but only two call-sites are described — A1's compact rename path and A3's fsck repair path. This does not meet the Rule-of-Three threshold (>=3 existing call-sites) for a shared abstraction and risks premature coupling of the repair operation into the compaction module.
findings.location: Scope section: '`_commands/compact.py` (shared retire helper)'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
